coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis of lignin-derived carbon dots with visible pH response for Fe3+ detection and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Simin
Lai, Chunmei
Huang, Zejie
Liu, Wei
Xiong, Lei
Wu, Yuxin
Jin, Yanqiao
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of lignin-based carbon dots (LCDs) with high quantum yield (QY), stable fluorescence properties and biocompatibility has been a challenge. Here, we propose an improved two-step strategy for producing high-quality LCDs from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EHL). The p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid used in the strategy not only provides nitrogen and sulfur elements, but also tailors the disordered three-dimensional structure of EHL. The successful co-doping of N and S elements favors the reduction of the optical energy bandgap (Eg), resulting in a high QY of 45.05% for LCDs. The LCDs exhibited superior selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μM when Fe3+ concentration was 50–500 μM. In addition, LCDs demonstrated significant fluorescence in HepG2 cells and HepG2 cells loaded with LCDs at a concentration of 80 μg/mL showed good viability, suggesting that they are suitable for in vivo applications. The luminescent centers of LCDs change during pH regulation and thus show a special visual response to pH changes, making them have great potential for detecting metabolism in living cells. This work provides a novel and low-cost method for fabricating sustainable fluorescent probes for chemical sensing and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Enzymatic hydrolysis lignin
Fe3+ detection
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing capability of LCDs at different pH values
Interference of pH and other ions on Fe3+ detection
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
Morphology and structure of the LCDs
Fluorescence characteristics of LCDs with different pH values
Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions
Interference of pH and other ions on Fe3+ detection
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of LCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province, China (Grant No. 2021 J01593 ) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of LCDs; FT-IR(b), XPS spectra(c) and high-resolution of C1s(d), N1s(e), O1s(f), and S2p(g) of EHL, LNPs and LCDs.

Major substructures identified in 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of EHL (a, b), LNPs (c, d) and LCDs (e, f): (A) and (A') β-aryl-ether linkages, (B) phenylcoumarane structure, (C) resin alcohol bond, (FA) ferulate, (pCA) para coumarate, (H)p-hydroxyphenyl, (G) and (G') guaiacyl unit, (S) and (S') syringyl.
The proposed formation mechanism of LCDs.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption, inset photos of LCDs under everyday light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (b) The fluorescence spectra of LCDs under different excitation wavelengths; (c) UV–Vis extinction spectra of LCDs solutions; (d) Diagram of the possible energy transition mechanism of CDs.

(a) Changes of emission wavelength and luminescence color of LCDs at different pH values; (b) Fluorescence intensity plot of LCDs cycling between pH 2–11 at 350 nm excitation; (c) Color change of LCDs solution at pH 2–11 under 365 nm UV light irradiation.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of LCDs with various metal ions; (b) Fluorescence spectra of LCDs with different Fe3+ concentrations (0–1000 µM); (c) Linear fit of 1-F/F0 for different Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 0–1000 µM and 50–500 µM (inset); (d) Fluorescence brightness of LCDs with various metal ions.
(a) Fluorescence decay of LCDs in the absence or presence of Fe3+ (Ex: 375 nm, Em: 476 nm); (b) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of LCDs only, LCDs in the presence of Fe3+ and Fe3+ only.
LCDs were incubated with HepG2 cells for 6 h. The CLSM images were (a) under bright field; (b) HepG2 cells (excited at 405 nm); (c) LCDs (excited at 405 nm); (d) overlapped images of b and c.
The quantum yield of biomass-based CDs.
LOD of biomass-based CDs for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Sustainable synthesis of lignin-derived carbon dots with visible pH response for Fe3+ detection and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian College Association Instrumental Analysis Center of Fuzhou University, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian College Association Instrumental Analysis Center of Fuzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian College Association Instrumental Analysis Center of Fuzhou University, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of lignin-based carbon dots (LCDs) with high quantum yield (QY), stable fluorescence properties and biocompatibility has been a challenge. Here, we propose an improved two-step strategy for producing high-quality LCDs from enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EHL). The p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid used in the strategy not only provides nitrogen and sulfur elements, but also tailors the disordered three-dimensional structure of EHL. The successful co-doping of N and S elements favors the reduction of the optical energy bandgap (Eg), resulting in a high QY of 45.05% for LCDs. The LCDs exhibited superior selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μM when Fe3+ concentration was 50–500 μM. In addition, LCDs demonstrated significant fluorescence in HepG2 cells and HepG2 cells loaded with LCDs at a concentration of 80 μg/mL showed good viability, suggesting that they are suitable for in vivo applications. The luminescent centers of LCDs change during pH regulation and thus show a special visual response to pH changes, making them have great potential for detecting metabolism in living cells. This work provides a novel and low-cost method for fabricating sustainable fluorescent probes for chemical sensing and bioimaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as one new type of subminiature photoluminescence (PL) nanomaterial, are promising candidates to replace toxic metal-based quantum dots [1] due to their exceptional physicochemical properties, such as excellent biocompatibility, optical stability, environmental friendliness and low toxicity, with potential applications in bioimaging[2], chemical sensors [3], optoelectronic devices[4], and catalysis [5]. In recent years, carbon precursors for the synthesis of CDs mainly are small organic molecules (chemicals) and biomass materials [6]. Citric acid [7], malic acid [8], and cysteine [9,10] are commonly used to synthesize CDs. However, nontoxic natural materials, such as magnolia liliiflora [11], cranberry beans [12], retusus fruits [13], chitosan [14], even lemon juice [15], etc. were used to synthesize CDs, which showed the advantages of low cost, environmental protection, and renewability and meet the requirements of the Carbon Peaking and Carbon Neutrality Goals. Therefore, biomass has become an ideal source and focus of attention for the fabrication of CDs.

Lignin is one of the three major components of plants and the most abundant natural polymer in the plant kingdom after cellulose. Nonetheless, lignin is almost the most difficult natural polymer to identify and exploit [16,17], and it was not really studied until after 1930. Lignin is often disposed of as industrial waste or processed directly to generate heat through an underutilized combustion process. However, combustion treatment is obviously a great waste of resources and will also increase the environmental burden. Therefore, it is urgent to carry out research on converting lignin into value-added products[18].

In fact, the carbon content of lignin is as high as 60%, and its inherently high defect structure favors the formation of CDs with graphene-like ordered structures [19,20]. More and more researchers choose lignin as a carbon precursor to compose LCDs. Alkali lignin, a by-product of the paper industry, is first subjected to be investigated. However, due to the wide range of various C-O and C–C bond strengths contained in alkali lignin [21,22] and the facile repolymerization of low molecular weight components, CDs from alkali lignin usually suffer from low QY, large particle size and inferior fluorescence properties. Researchers hence exploited heteroatom doping to regulate the structure of electronic energy levels and then improve the quantum yield of lignin-derived CDs. For example, N element can be introduced into the alkali lignin framework via Mannich and Machael addition reactions to form amine-rich LCDs with a quantum yield of 8%[23]. Carbonization of kraft (alkali) lignin using mixed acid (H2SO4: HNO3 = 1:3, v/v) as N and S dopants by compressed liquid CO2 antisolvent method, yielding LCDs with a QY of 13% [24]. In view of the green and non-toxic characteristics of the raw materials and fabrication processes for preparing LCDs, as well as satisfactory water solubility and fluorescence properties, they can be used as chemical sensors for ion detection or cells biosensing platforms for imaging [23–26].

In this work, we proposed a sustainable “two-step” strategy to fabricate high-quality LCDs using enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EHL) as the raw material. To overcome the complex structure of EHL and the low quantum yield of LCDs, we used p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid as the scissor and nitrogen-sulfur dopant. N and S elements were introduced during EHL cracking to obtain lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) with nitrogen- and sulfur-containing groups on the surface. The LNPs were further subjected to green and facile hydrothermal carbonization to produce the target LCDs. By means of XPS, FT-IR, 2D HSQC NMR, the preparation mechanism of LCDs using the “two-step” strategy was revealed, and the possible quenching mechanism of Fe3+ was deduced. Furthermore, the pH visualization response and cycling, optical bandgap, ion selectivity and sensitivity of LCDs were also investigated. Herein, we introduce a novel synthetic method of high-quality LCDs, and the results are expected to provide some valuable guidance for researchers engaged in the field of biomass or carbon dots.

EHL was provided by Longlive Bio-technology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China), and it was washed with excess deionized water to neutral, filtered, and then dried. p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, KCl, FeSO4⋅7H2O, NiCl2⋅6H2O, MgCl2⋅6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl3, CuCl2⋅2H2O, NaCl, CaCl2⋅6H2O, CoCl2⋅6H2O, MgCl2, NiCl2⋅6H2O used in this work were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dialysis bags with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da were produced by American Biomed Instruments Inc. The mixed water filter membrane with a pore size of 0.45 µm was provided by Taoyuan Medical Chemical Instrument Factory. All the regents referred above were analytically pure and used without further treatment. Human hepatocellular liver carcinoma cell lines (HepG2 cells) were purchased from the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China), fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) were purchased from Life Technologies.

The p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid was completely dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, and the resulting solution was poured into a 250 mL three-neck flask and placed in a 90 °C water bath. Subsequently, EHL was added into the three-necked flask and stirred for 1 h. After that, the resulting solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm water filter to acquire a solution containing lignin nanoparticles (LNPs). Then, the LNPs solution was reacted in a 25 mL hydrothermal kettle and placed in an oven with a set temperature of 200 °C for 20 h to achieve the LCDs solution. Next, LCDs solution was filtered using a sterile syringe attached to a 0.22 μm filter to remove larger particle size impurities. The obtained filtrate was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 48 h to gain the target solution of LCDs.

The surface functional groups of LCDs were analyzed by utilizing Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and K-Alpha+ spectrometer equipped with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation (1350.08 eV). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded adopting a Lambda950 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Fluorescence spectra were determined with a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, France). TEM images were observed by a Talos F200i transmission electron microscopy at 200 kV. The imaging of cells was obtained by employing a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, TCS SP8, Leica, Germany).

The fluorescence QY of LCDs was characterized by the integration method, which was connected to the integration sphere of the FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer for testing [27]. Quantum cuvette calibration was selected as the calibration file for spherical calibration. Based on the fluorescence spectra of the sample and deionized water (as blank background) at the optimal excitation wavelength, the QY of the LCDs could be calculated using the fluorescence software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing capability of LCDs at different pH values
ce:section.ce:para: Solutions of LCDs with different pH values (2.0–11.0) were prepared using 0.1 M HCl and NaOH solutions as the pH regulator. The fluorescence spectra of LCDs solutions with different pH values were measured by the fluorescence spectrometer. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of LCDs and pH was explored hence.

Selectivity studies included adding different ionic solutions (Fe2+, Pb2+, K+, Fe3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, 50 mM) to LCDs solutions respectively and comparing the fluorescence intensity under the optimal excitation wavelength. To further evaluate the sensitivity of LCDs towards Fe3+, different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–1000 μM) were added into the prepared LCDs solution for fluorescence spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference of pH and other ions on Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of LCDs solution was adjusted to 2,6 and 11, separately, with 0.1 M HCl and NaOH as the regulators. Subsequently, 20 μL of 50 mM metal ions (Fe2+, Pb2+, K+, Fe3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+) were added into LCDs solutions with different pH, respectively, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. In order to investigate the interference of metal ions, various ionic ions (Fe2+, Pb2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+) were added to the LCDs solution containing Fe3+ and the change of fluorescence intensity was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practicability of LCDs, the experiments of fluorescence detection of Fe3+ were conducted using tap water as the base medium. Different concentrations of Fe3+ solution (100, 150, 200 and 300 μM) were prepared with tap water and added to LCDs solution. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. The data in this experiment were averaged after three measurements.

Cell culture: HepG2 cells were cultured in DMEM medium containing 1% penicillin/streptomycin and 10% FBS at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2. Cellular uptake of LCDs was investigated using cell imaging experiments.

MTT assays: The cytotoxicity of LCDs was assessed by MTT [3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazolyl-2)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] assay. Briefly, cells were seeded and the previous medium was replaced with 100 mL of fresh medium containing different amounts of LCDs (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 μg/mL) and incubated for 24 h, followed by MTT (100 μg/mL, 100 μL) for 4 h. Next, added 100 µL of DMSO to each plate to dissolve the formazan crystals. Finally, cell viability was assessed by TECAN Infinite F200 microplate reader.

Cell imaging: Cell imaging experiments were conducted to evaluate the cellular uptake of synthesized LCDs. Specifically, the cells were placed on a 12 well plate (3 × 105 per well) overnight until adherent. Next, thoroughly wash the previous culture medium with PBS and use a medium containing 50 μg/mL of LCDs in DMEM solution for 4 h. The cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min. Finally, images of cells were captured using the CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from the TEM image (Fig. 1 a), the as-prepared LCDs are zero-dimensional, uniformly sized, and well-dispersed spherical nanoparticles. The particle size of the LCDs was small and the distribution range was narrow, ranging from 1 to 6 nm, with an average diameter of 3.55 nm. As illustrated by the FT-IR spectra of EHL, LNPs and LCDs in Fig. 1b, the rich aromatic structure of EHL could be revealed by the aromatic C = C framework vibrational bands around 1450–1640 cm−1. LNPs and LCDs better inherited the aromatic structure of EHL. The absorption peaks of EHL at 1128 cm−1 and 1256 cm−1 were caused by aliphatic ethers and aromatic ethers, respectively, indicating that EHL contains abundant oxygen-containing groups [10]. The absorption bands at 1128–1180 cm−1 represented the stretching vibrations of -SO2 in aromatic sulfonic acids (Ar-SO2-OH) and aliphatic sulfonic acids (R-SO2-OH). Besides, the stretching vibrational energy bands of LNPs and LCDs at 600–900 cm−1 were mainly derived from the stretching vibrations of C-S and S-O, demonstrating the successful doping of S element [28].

The full scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1c) manifested the presence of N and S elements in LNPs and LCDs that were not present in EHL, indicating successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur, which is consistent with the conclusion drawn by FT-IR. The XPS high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p of EHL, LNP and LCDs were shown in Fig. 1d-g. Among them, the C1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. 1d) all had the strongest binding energy peak at 284.6 eV, proving that their carbon structures were still dominated by C = C and C–C [29]. The O1s high-resolution spectrum of EHL has the strongest binding energy peak at 531.8 eV (Fig. 1e), which is assigned to the C-O-C/C-OH structure. However, the C-O-C and C-OH binding peaks in the O1s of LNPs and LCDs disappeared, suggesting that C-O-C cleavage and dehydration condensation occurred. Pyridine nitrogen C-N-C, pyrrolic nitrogen C2-N–H and graphitic nitrogen C3-N groups were displayed in the N1s band (Fig. 1f), which was a proof of the successful N doping into the carbon framework of LCDs [9,15,30]. Due to the energy level splitting of the high-resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. 1g), double peaks appeared at 168.9 eV (S2p3/2) and 170.1 eV (S2p1/2), respectively, representing the successful introduction of -SO2 and -SO3 group in LCDs [31]. The introduction of these hydrophilic groups –COOH, -SO2, –NH2 resulted in enhanced water solubility of LCDs.

The structural changes of EHL, LNPs and LCDs during the preparation process were tested by 2D HSQC NMR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). HSQC image of EHL demonstrated that its side chain region (50–90/3.2–5 ppm) had methoxy group (OCH3), β-aryl-ether linkages (A) and (A'), phenylcoumarane structure (B) and resinol bond (C) signals, verifying that it was a disordered polymer with a complex three-dimensional network structure. In addition, the aromatic region (100–135/6.0–7.8 ppm) of EHL was apparent in signals, which were ferulate (FA), p-coumarate (pCA), p-hydroxyphenyl(H), guaiac-based unit(G), syringyl (S and S') [32]. After acid hydrolysis, the signal intensity of the aliphatic regions of LNPs and LCDs decreased significantly relative to EHL, which is consistent with the analysis in XPS, further demonstrating that C-O-C and C–C cleavage occurred during acid hydrolysis and the H, pCA, FA and G' structure of EHL remained in the aromatic region.

Based on the above experimental evidences, the possible mechanism for the synthesis of LCDs from EHL via a two-step method was illustrated (Fig. 3 ). The C-O-C or C–C bonds of EHL were cleaved during acid hydrolysis by using the triple action of p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid as a “top-down” scissor, dopant, and surfactant [33]. Meanwhile, more functional groups containing oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur were introduced, resulting in more active sites on the surface of LNPs, which was beneficial for subsequent synthesis. Then, during the hydrothermal treatment of the LNPs solution, aromatic remelting occurred to increase sp2 hybridization, nitrogen atoms were successfully introduced into the carbon backbone, and the surface of the target LCDs still possessed oxygen-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-functional groups, giving them excellent fluorescence properties. Furthermore, the control experiment revealed that the QY of the product was only 1.66% when p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid was not used in the system. This also proves that p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid actually plays an important role in the synthesis of high quality LCDs.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of LCDs had two exciton adsorption bands located at 255 nm and 353 nm. The prominent absorption peak at 255 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain, which implied that the LCDs formed the conjugated backbone. The absorption peak at 353 nm was an energy trap caused by the n-π* transition of the functional group (COOH/C-N/C-S). As revealed by the inset in Fig. 4a, the LCDs solution exhibited a bright blue color under UV light at 365 nm, while it was a clear pale yellow solution under ordinary light [34].It could be seen from Fig. 4b that the fluorescence intensity of LCDs first increased and then decreased within the wavelength range of 320–430 nm. Obviously, 350 nm was the optimal excitation wavelength, and LCDs showed the strongest emission under the excitation of 350 nm. Additionally, the LCDs with nitrogen-sulfur co-doping had excitation-independent emission phenomena, which might be due to the fact that the introduced sulfur atoms seem to eliminate the O-states and enhance the N-state, resulting in the pristine surface states that were almost ignored in LCDs[9].

Using a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer, the fluorescence yield of the LCDs was calculated to be as high as 45.05% at the optimal excitation wavelength of 350 nm after calibration with a quanta phi cuvette correction file. The QY calibration diagram of fluorescence spectrophotometer was exhibited in Fig. S1. It can be seen from Table 1 that CDs synthesized from biomass as precursors usually have the problem of low QY, especially lignin, which is easy to re-aggregate with low molecular components and has a complex structure. In order to better prove that this method is an effective means to improve the QY of LCDs, LCDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of EHL and p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid under the same conditions. The resulting QY was 5.20%. Through the experimental comparison, it can be seen that the “top-down” method was used to pretreat the EHL to crack it and accompanied by the occurrence of N, S co-doping, which could produce LCDs with high QY.

To further demonstrate that the favorable fluorescence performance of LCDs is related to nitrogen-sulfur doping, we calculated the optical bandgap energy (Eg) using the formula Eg = hc/λcut-off, where h is the Planck constant (6.626 × 10-34 J·s), c is the speed of light (3.0 × 108 m/s), and λcut-off denotes the onset of the first wave in the exciton absorption band[38]. Eg of the LCDs was calculated to be 2.67 eV (Fig. 4c). The energy conversion mechanism of LCDs is shown in Fig. 4d. Under the excitation of light, the formed electrons and holes further reached Eg from their respective HOMO and LUMO level through the relaxation process. The excited states associated with this transition energy were simultaneously filled to radiate recombination and emit different photoluminescence spectra. It has been reported previously that undoped LCDs have a consequent poor fluorescence performance due to the small number of edge states and large Eg. Lower Eg of successfully doped LCDs leads to easier radiative recombination upon photoexcitation and thus corresponds to better fluorescence behavior [39]. From Table 2 , it can be seen that the Eg of LCDs is significantly reduced after doping with N and S, which is beneficial to the improvement of the fluorescence properties and QY [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characteristics of LCDs with different pH values
ce:section.ce:para: The pH responsiveness of LCDs was evaluated by fluorescence spectra of LCDs solutions at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when the pH was between 2 and 6, the emission peak positions of LCDs under excitation at 350 nm were basically unchanged and located in the blue region. With increasing NaOH addition, the LCDs developed a unique pH response with a blue-shifted emission peak at 350 nm excitation. The reason was that the surface of LCDs was rich in nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups, and hydroxide groups in NaOH were combined with the surface of LCDs through hydrogen bonds to form new stable luminescence centers, resulting in the luminescence spectrum shifting into navy blue light emission.

The fluorescence intensity stability during repeated pH stimuli is of significant for pH-responsive LCDs. The pH was adjusted repeatedly between 2 and 11 with 4 cycles (Fig. 5b). It is worth noting that although the intensity decreased after multiple cycles, it remained above 80% after the fourth cycle, and the luminescence was still obvious. It can be then determined that the LCDs not only exhibit visual pH responsiveness (Fig. 5c), but also maintain relatively stable fluorescence characteristics when pH stimulation is repeatedly adjusted[43]. Studies have shown that the pH of the cellular microenvironment of human tumor cells (4.3–5.2) is lower than that of normal human cells (∼7.4) due to altered cellular metabolism[44]. Since LCDs have special fluorescence responses with different emission colors at pH 2–11, they can be used as proton sensors to detect changes in cellular metabolism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The original LCDs solution (pH = 3) was used for the selectivity and sensitivity test of ion detection. In order to investigate the ion selectivity of LCDs, different ion solutions (Fe2+, Pb2+, K+, Fe3+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, 50 mM) were added into the LCDs solution respectively. Their fluorescence spectra under excitation of 350 nm were compared. As shown in Fig. 6 a, only the fluorescence intensity of the LCDs solution added with Fe3+ was significantly quenched, and the quenching effect of other ions was almost negligible, which indicated that the LCDs solution had a high selectivity for Fe3+.

The fluorescence intensities of LCDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions were also investigated to evaluate their sensitivity and the limit of detection (LOD). The results (see Fig. 6b) displayed that the fluorescence intensity of LCDs centered at 350 nm gradually decreased with the increasing Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 1000 µM. Fig. 6c represented the relationship between the quenching efficiency 1-F/F0 (F0 represented the fluorescence intensity without Fe3+ addition, F represents the fluorescence intensity with Fe3+ addition) and the Fe3+ concentration added in the LCDs solution. The inset in Fig. 6c manifested that at low concentration of Fe3+ addition (50–500 μM), the change of the fluorescence intensity showed a good linearity, and the square of the correlation coefficient (R2) reached 0.9956. By calculation, the LOD of LCDs was as low as 0.15 μM based on the equation LOD = 3δ/k, where δ and k represent the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 3) and the slope of the linear fit line, respectively. The picture of LCDs solution added with different ions under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp was illustrated in Fig. 6d. Among them, the fluorescence brightness of the LCDs solution with Fe3+ addition was obviously darkened. As listed in Table 3 , the LOD for Fe3+ detected by the LCDs was lower than that of CDs achieved from other biomasses. Furthermore, the LOD is also lower than the maximum level of comprehensive wastewater discharge (0.5 mg/L), aquatic animals and their products (2 mg/kg) allowed by Chinese national standards, and the highest level of drinking water allowed by WHO (0.962 μM) [45], indicating that LCDs had satisfactory detection potential for Fe3+ in real samples.

The possible explanation for the significant quenching effect of Fe3+ on LCDs was speculated. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence lifetime of LCDs with Fe3+ (τ1: 4.83 ns, τ2: 22.47 ns) is very close to that without Fe3+ (τ1: 4.51 ns, τ2: 22.01 ns). LCDs have a strong absorption peak at 255 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectrum (see Fig. 7b), while Fe3+ exhibited a strong absorption at 300 nm. With the addition of Fe3+, the absorption peak intensity of LCDs at 255 nm was significantly weakened, which might be due to the complexation reaction between LCDs and Fe3+, thereby producing non-fluorescent substances[49]. The above results indicated that static quenching occurred between LCDs and Fe3+ [50–52]. Fe3+ has a high affinity for N and O elements, so that it can coordinate with the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of LCDs or generate non-luminescent ground-state complexes to quench the fluorescence [53,54]. Moreover, the introduction of electronegative nitrogen atoms can excite the transfer of electrons from the surface of LCDs to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions, resulting in non-radiative electron-hole recombination, and energy is not released in the form of photons [55,56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference of pH and other ions on Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the complexity of the actual environment detection, the anti-interference experiment of LCDs as Fe3+ probe was carried out. The selectivity of LCDs to Fe3+ at different pH and the change of fluorescence intensity after adding different metal ions were investigated. As illustrated in a1-a3 of Fig. S2, LCDs still have significant selectivity for Fe3+ even if the pH changes. Moreover, the quenching effect of Fe3+ on LCDs was almost unaffected by the addition of other ions (Fig. S2 b1-b3). These results indicate that LCDs have good anti-interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the reliability of the established sensing detection method, LCDs were evaluated by detecting Fe3+ in spiked tap water samples. When different concentrations of Fe3+ (100, 150, 200 and 300 μM) were added to the tap water sample, the detection of Fe3+ concentration was realized from the change of fluorescence intensity of LCDs. The practical application results are shown in Table S1. The average recovery was 97.98–101.17%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD%) was less than 1%. These results confirmed that the synthesized LCDs exhibited a high selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of Fe3+ in actual samples. Therefore, LCDs are potential fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: LCDs can be used as bioimaging agents due to their small size, high fluorescence intensity, and outstanding photostability. The cellular imaging capability of LCDs was validated using HepG2 cell experiments. Specifically, HepG2 cells were incubated with LCDs for 6 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere, after which cell images were acquired using CLSM with a laser channel of 405 nm. As suggested in Fig. 8 a-d, LCDs entered HepG2 cells via endocytosis and exhibited significant fluorescence cells indicating the high photoluminescence intensity and excellent biocompatibility of LCDs. The cytotoxicity of LCDs was investigated by MTT assay[57]. Besides the high fluorescence intensity and biocompatibility, the cytotoxicity of LCDs is also a key feature to evaluate whether they can be used in practical biomedical applications. As presented in Fig. S3, LCDs exhibited dose-dependent cytotoxicity. As the concentration of LCDs rose from 0 μg/mL to 80 μg/mL, there was a slight decrease in the activity of HepG2 cells. Nevertheless, it was noteworthy that over 80% of the cells survived even at the highest concentration of 80 μg/mL. In short, the satisfactory biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of LCDs were confirmed, which is evidence that they are promising candidates for fluorescent bioimaging agents.

An enhanced strategy for the synthesis of high-quality LCDs using EHL as carbon precursor has been proposed, breaking the QY barriers of CDs using biomass as carbon precursor. The p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid not only introduce N and S elements as the nitrogen-sulfur dopants, but also acts as a scissors to cut EHL. The aromatics are then remelted to produce LCDs with QY up to 45.05%. Benefiting from the high sensitivity and selectivity, LCDs can be used as fluorescent nanoprobes for Fe3+ detection. Even when Fe3+ concentration was low (50–500 μM), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of LCDs demonstrated a good linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration (R2 = 0.9956), and LOD was as low as 0.15 μM. When LCDs was used for tap water detection, the recoveries of Fe3+ ions were 97.98–101.17%, with RSDs of less than 1%, which proved the practicability of sensitive detection of Fe3+ in real water samples. Furthermore, under 405 nm laser excitation in HepG2 cells, LCDs displayed a uniform distribute and stable emission of light. When the loading concentration of LCDs was 80 μg/mL, the survival rate of cells was over 80%, which was mainly attributed to the low cytotoxicity of LCDs. In conclusion, the LCDs have great potential to serve as fluorescent probes for biological imaging and chemical sensing.

Simin Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Chunmei Lai: Investigation, Methodology. Zejie Huang: Investigation. Wei Liu: Investigation. Lei Xiong: Comments & editing. Yuxin Wu: Supervising & editing. Yanqiao Jin: Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province, China (Grant No. 2021 J01593) .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123111.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
